title: n8n Training Tutorial
description: Step-by-step guide to mastering n8n workflows

content.value: n8n is an open-source workflow automation platform. Build workflows by connecting nodes in a visual editor. Each node performs an action (call API, transform data, send message), and data flows between nodes as JSON.

Key Features

  • Data = JSON — Every node receives and returns JSON objects
  • Open-source & Self-host — Run on your server, full data control
  • No-code + Code — Visual editor or write JavaScript/Python
  • 400+ Integrations — Gmail, Slack, HTTP, databases, and more
A workflow is a sequence of connected nodes that process data. Trigger starts execution → Data flows through connections → Each node transforms data → Output.

content.alt: Hello World workflow in n8n visual editor showing two connected nodes

content.value: Nodes are the building blocks of n8n workflows. Each node has inputs, outputs, and configuration parameters.

Common Node Types

Node TypeKey QuestionExamples
Trigger NodeWhen does the workflow run?Webhook, Schedule, Gmail Trigger, Manual Trigger
Action NodeWhat to do with the data?Google Sheets, Slack, HTTP Request, Gmail Send, Edit Fields
Logic NodeHow does data flow?IF (branching), Merge (combine), Code (JS/Python)

content.alt: Edit Fields node configuration panel in n8n showing parameters and output

content.value:
Node Structure

  • name: Human-readable node name
  • type: Node type identifier (n8n-nodes-base.set)
  • position: Visual placement on canvas [x, y]
  • parameters: Configuration settings
  • id: Unique identifier
Every node passes JSON to the next node. Connect nodes together and you create a data pipeline.

content.alt: User processing workflow showing data flow through multiple nodes

content.value: The Data Journey: Create Sample Users → Split Into Items (1 array becomes 2 items) → Transform Data (add new fields to each item)
Expressions in Action

3 Rules to Remember

RuleExplanation
1 item = 1 JSON objectEach object is processed separately through the workflow. If you have 100 items, the workflow runs 100 times internally.
Array = batch processingn8n loops through items automatically—no extra code needed. The Split Into Items node converts an array into individual items.
Access with expressionsUse {{ $json.email }} to get "alice@test.com" for the first item. The $json variable always refers to the current item being processed.
Expressions let you access and manipulate data. Wrap JavaScript in {{ }} to access fields, do math, use methods, or build logic.
Expression Syntax

{{ $json.fieldName }}       // Access a field
{{ $json.age + 5 }}          // Do math
{{ $json.name.toUpperCase() }} // Use JavaScript methods
{{ $json.role === 'admin' ? 'Full Access' : 'Limited' }} // Conditionals
Variable Types

VariableWhat It DoesWhen to Use
$jsonData from the previous nodeMost common—use by default
$('Node Name')Data from any specific nodeWhen you need data from a node that's not directly before
$inputCurrent node's input dataAdvanced—loops, multiple inputs
$now / $todayCurrent date/timeTimestamps, date filtering, logging
Examples

content.alt: Complete workflow showing all 5 nodes with expression outputs in the data table
Add Metadata node detail view showing input data, expression parameters, and output results

content.value: Logic nodes control where data flows. Route items down different paths based on conditions, then merge back together.
IF Node — Two paths: true or false

// Example conditions:
{{ $json.role === 'admin' }}              // Is user an admin?
{{ $json.age >= 18 }}                     // Is user 18 or older?
{{ $json.price > 100 }}                   // Is price over $100?
{{ $json.status === 'active' }}           // Is status active?
{{ $json.email.includes('@company.com') }} // Is company email?
Switch Node — Multiple paths based on value
Merge Node — Combine multiple paths back into one

Comparison

NodeOutputsUse When
IF2 (true/false)Binary decision — yes or no, include or exclude
SwitchMultipleRoute by value — status, category, type, etc.
Merge1Combine paths back together after branching
Filter1Remove items that don't match condition

content.alt: Complete workflow showing branching, merging, and Gmail integration with 7 nodes
Split Into Items node converting 1 array into 3 separate user items
IF node detail showing condition check and true/false branch outputs
Merge node showing append mode combining 2 admin items and 1 user item into 3 total items
Gmail node showing HTML message template and successful email sends for all 3 users
Actual Gmail inbox showing the User Access Summary Report with all 3 users listed with their access levels and badges

content.value: Production workflows handle errors gracefully. Use Continue on Fail, Error Triggers, and validation to catch failures and provide visibility.

Error Types

Error TypeExampleSolution
API FailureExternal service returns 500 errorRetry logic, fallback data source
Missing DataExpected field is undefinedDefault values, validation checks
Invalid InputEmail format is incorrectValidation before processing
TimeoutRequest takes too longIncrease timeout, optimize query
Error Trigger Node — Runs when any node fails
Continue on Fail — Node setting to prevent failure from stopping workflow
Validation with IF Node

Debugging Techniques

TechniqueHow To
Inspect Node OutputClick node → View output tab to see actual data
Pin DataRight-click node → Pin data to test with sample data
Execute Single NodeClick node → Execute node to test in isolation
Check Execution LogView execution history to see what went wrong
Use Edit Fields to LogAdd debug fields to see intermediate values
Common Pattern

// Pattern: Validate → Try → Catch → Notify

1. IF Node: Validate input
   ├─ Valid → Continue to main workflow
   └─ Invalid → Send to error handler

2. Enable "Continue On Fail" on risky nodes
   - API calls
   - External integrations
   - File operations

3. Error Trigger workflow:
   - Log error details
   - Send notification (Slack/Email)
   - Store failed items for retry

4. IF Node: Check for errors
   ├─ Success → Continue
   └─ Failed → Fallback action or skip

content.alt: Successful API call showing data flowing through the true branch with user data displayed in output table
Failed API call showing error object flowing through the false branch with error details
Gmail inbox showing the automated error alert email with error details and timestamp

content.value:
Best Practices

  • Fail fast — Validate input early, before expensive operations
  • Be specific — Check for exact error conditions rather than catching all errors
  • Provide context — Include useful info in error messages (execution ID, failed node, input data)
  • Test failure paths — Don't just test the happy path; simulate failures
  • Monitor & alert — Set up Error Trigger workflows for critical workflows
AI APIs let you analyze, generate, and transform data using LLMs. Send text in → Get intelligent response back.

AI Providers

ProviderModelsBest For
OpenAIGPT-4, GPT-3.5General purpose, JSON mode, function calling
AnthropicClaude 4Long context, analysis, reasoning
GoogleGeminiMultimodal (text + images), fast
Open SourceLlama, MistralSelf-hosted, cost control
Text Generation

Parameters

ParameterWhat It Does
ModelWhich AI to use (gpt-4 = smart but slow, gpt-3.5 = fast but cheaper)
TemperatureCreativity (0 = consistent, 1 = creative). Use 0 for data extraction, 0.7 for writing.
Max TokensResponse length limit. 1 token ≈ 0.75 words. Limits cost.
System MessageSets AI behavior ("You are a helpful customer service agent")
Chat vs Completion
Response Handling

content.alt: Complete AI email responder workflow with 5 nodes: Manual Trigger, Sample Email, Analyze Sentiment, Generate Response, and Format Output
Analyze Sentiment node showing OpenAI configuration with prompt to classify email sentiment as positive, negative, or neutral
Generate Response node with system message and user prompt, showing AI-generated professional customer service email response

content.value: Embeddings convert text into numbers (vectors) that capture meaning. Similar concepts have similar vectors, enabling semantic search beyond keyword matching.
How It Works
Creating Embeddings

content.alt: OpenAI Embeddings API single text request in Insomnia showing input text and embedding response
OpenAI Embeddings API batch request in Insomnia showing array of texts and embedding response

content.value:
Vector Databases

DatabaseBest ForNotes
PineconeProduction, managed serviceFast, scalable, fully managed
WeaviateSelf-hosted, open sourceGraphQL API, hybrid search
QdrantHigh performance, filteringFast, good for metadata filters
Supabase VectorPostgreSQL extensionpgvector, familiar SQL interface

Measuring Similarity with Cosine Distance

Let's see how embeddings capture meaning by comparing two different ways of describing the same login issue and calculating their similarity.

content.alt: Insomnia showing Embedding 1 request with input text: The user is experiencing login issues with two-factor authentication
Insomnia showing Embedding 2 request with input text: The customer cannot sign in because of two-step verification problem
Claude Code showing cosine similarity calculation result: 0.648507 (64.85%) - moderate similarity with semantic overlap

content.value:
Cosine Similarity: 0.648507 (64.85%)

Interpretation: Moderate similarity - The two inputs share semantic overlap despite using completely different words:

  • "user" vs "customer" - different terms for the same concept
  • "login issues" vs "cannot sign in" - same meaning, different phrasing
  • "two-factor authentication" vs "two-step verification" - technical synonyms

This demonstrates how embeddings capture meaning rather than just matching keywords. A traditional keyword search would miss this connection!

content.alt: Qdrant UI showing both embeddings stored as Point 1 and Point 2 with their source metadata and input text, each with 1536-dimension vectors

content.value:
Storing in Vector Database (Qdrant)

After calculating similarity, we can store these embeddings in a vector database for future semantic search:

content.alt: Claude Code showing the execution plan for generating 20 diverse embeddings with expected similarity patterns

content.value:
How We Generated 20 Diverse Embeddings

Using Claude and Insomnia MCP, we created a rich dataset to visualize semantic similarity:

  1. Claude generated diverse sentences across 7 categories:
    • Authentication & Login: 3 texts
    • Payment & Billing: 3 texts
    • Shipping & Delivery: 3 texts
    • Product Features: 3 texts
    • Technical Support: 3 texts
    • Account Management: 3 texts
    • General Inquiries: 2 texts
  2. Insomnia MCP executed OpenAI embedding requests for each text
  3. All vectors saved to Qdrant with metadata (source, input_text, category)

This diverse dataset demonstrates how embeddings capture semantic meaning across different topics and categories.

content.alt: Qdrant HNSW graph showing 20 embeddings as colored dots with connections, demonstrating semantic clustering by category

content.value:
HNSW Graph Visualization

After uploading 20 diverse embeddings to Qdrant, we can visualize them in the HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World) graph. Each dot represents one embedding, and the connections show semantic relationships:

  • Colors represent categories: Cyan (shipping, features, general), Orange (auth, payment, tech, account)
  • Distance = Similarity: Closer dots have more similar meanings
  • Connections: Lines show the nearest neighbors in the vector space
  • Selected point (Point 10): "How do I export my data to CSV format" (feature category)

Key Concepts

  • Embedding dimension: Usually 1536 (OpenAI text-embedding-3-small) or 768 (smaller models)
  • Top-k search: Return k most similar results (typically 3-10) based on vector distance
  • HNSW: Graph structure for fast approximate nearest neighbor search, visualizes semantic relationships
  • Vector distance: Cosine distance measures angle between vectors; closer = more similar meaning

RAG combines vector search with LLMs to answer questions using your own data. Instead of relying on the LLM's training data, you retrieve relevant context from your knowledge base and inject it into the prompt.
How RAG Works

// Traditional LLM (no RAG):
User: "What's our password reset policy?"
LLM: "I don't have access to your company's specific policies..." ❌

// With RAG:
User: "What's our password reset policy?"
  → 1. Convert query to embedding
  → 2. Search vector DB for similar docs
  → 3. Retrieve top 3 matching chunks
  → 4. Inject chunks into LLM prompt as context
  → 5. LLM answers using YOUR data ✅

LLM Response: "According to your policy, users can reset passwords
by clicking 'Forgot Password' on the login page. A reset link is
sent within 5 minutes. For security, links expire after 1 hour."

// The LLM now has access to your specific knowledge!

Semantic Search Pattern

Before we build a full RAG system, let's understand how semantic search works - the foundation of RAG retrieval.
Why Chunking is Required

Before creating embeddings, you must split large documents into smaller chunks. Three reasons:

  1. Model token limits - Embedding models have max input size (e.g., 8191 tokens for text-embedding-ada-002)
  2. Retrieval precision - Smaller chunks = more precise semantic matches. A 50-page manual shouldn't be one embedding.
  3. Context window efficiency - Only inject relevant chunks into LLM, not entire documents
How Chunking Works
Chunking Best Practices

// Key considerations when chunking:

1. Chunk Size
   Too small (50 words):  ❌ Loses context, fragments meaning
   Too large (2000 words): ❌ Less precise retrieval, wastes tokens
   Sweet spot: 200-500 words ✅
   
2. Overlap Between Chunks
   No overlap:     ❌ Info at boundaries gets split, context lost
   10-20% overlap: ✅ Prevents boundary issues
   
   Example:
   Chunk 1: [words 1-500]
   Chunk 2: [words 450-950]  // 50 words overlap
   Chunk 3: [words 900-1400] // 50 words overlap

3. Semantic Boundaries
   ❌ Don't split mid-sentence: "The password must be at least 12 char" | "acters long"
   ✅ Split at paragraphs or section breaks
   ✅ Keep related concepts together

4. Content Type Matters
   Code:       Use function/class boundaries
   Markdown:   Split by ## headings
   Tables:     Keep entire table in one chunk
   JSON/YAML:  Keep complete objects together
RAG Workflow Pattern

// Phase 1: Index Your Knowledge Base (one-time setup)

Manual Trigger / Schedule
  → Load Documents
      (from files, URLs, databases, APIs)
  → Split Into Chunks
      (500 words, 20% overlap, respect boundaries)
  → For each chunk:
      → Create Embedding (OpenAI)
      → Store in Vector DB (with metadata)

// Phase 2: RAG Query (every user question)

Webhook / Manual Trigger
  → Get User Question
  → Create Question Embedding (OpenAI)
  → Search Vector DB
      (top 3-5 most similar chunks)
  → Build Context Prompt:
      "Answer based on this context:\n\n"
      + chunk_1 + "\n\n"
      + chunk_2 + "\n\n"
      + chunk_3 + "\n\n"
      + "Question: " + user_question
  → Send to LLM (OpenAI Message a Model)
  → Return Answer

// The LLM now has your specific knowledge injected as context!

Example: Building a RAG System with Shakespeare Text

Let's build a complete RAG system using the Tiny Shakespeare dataset. We'll index ~1MB of Shakespeare's works and enable semantic search.
Indexing Workflow
Workflow Steps:

1. Manual Trigger
   Click to start the workflow

2. HTTP Request
   Fetch Shakespeare text from GitHub
   URL: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/char-rnn/master/data/tinyshakespeare/input.txt
   Returns: ~1MB plain text

3. Code in JavaScript - Split into Chunks
   Input: Full text (~1MB)
   Chunk size: 1000 characters
   Overlap: 200 characters (20%)
   Output: ~1200 chunks

4. Code in JavaScript1 - Create Batches
   Input: 1200 chunks
   Batch size: 50 chunks per batch
   Output: ~24 batches
   Why? OpenAI embeddings API accepts multiple inputs

5. HTTP Request1 - Create Embeddings (runs 24 times)
   POST https://api.openai.com/v1/embeddings
   Model: text-embedding-3-small
   Input: Array of 50 texts
   Output: Array of 50 embeddings (1536 dimensions each)

6. Code in JavaScript2 - Prepare Qdrant Points
   Combine chunks with their embeddings
   Create point structure for Qdrant

7. HTTP Request2 - Store in Qdrant (runs 24 times)
   PUT https://[qdrant-url]/collections/tinyshakespeare/points
   Stores 50 points per request
   Total: ~1200 searchable chunks

Result: Shakespeare text fully indexed and ready for semantic search!

content.alt: RAG indexing workflow showing 7 nodes: Manual Trigger, HTTP Request to fetch Shakespeare text, Code to split into chunks, Code to create batches, HTTP Request to OpenAI embeddings, Code to prepare Qdrant points, and HTTP Request to store in Qdrant
Qdrant UI showing tinyshakespeare collection with stored points, displaying Point 1 and Point 2 with their chunk_index and Shakespeare text payloads

content.value:
Stored Points in Qdrant:

  • Collection: tinyshakespeare
  • Vector size: 1536 dimensions (text-embedding-3-small)
  • Distance: Cosine similarity
  • Total points: ~1200 chunks
  • Payload: chunk_index (number) + text (string)
Query Workflow
// Coming soon: Query workflow implementation

Common Patterns

  • Hybrid search: Combine vector similarity + keyword filters (e.g., only search "billing" category)
  • Re-ranking: Use a second model to re-order results for better relevance
  • Citation: Include source metadata in response (article title, URL, page number)
  • Fallback: If similarity score < 0.7, respond "I don't have information about that"
  • Conversational RAG: Include chat history in embedding for context-aware answers

Key Metrics to Monitor

  • Retrieval accuracy: Are the right chunks being found?
  • Answer quality: Are LLM responses accurate and helpful?
  • Latency: Embedding + search + LLM should be < 3 seconds
  • Cost: Track embedding API calls + vector DB queries + LLM tokens
